CURRENT AFFAIRS.

- Mr. Yeats appears to be rather upset by the with-
- drawal of Mr. Boyle’s plays. Perhaps his excitement
nay extenuate, though it cannot excuse the wildness
of one of his statements. A report of an interview
with him appeared in the ¢ Freeman’’ of Monday.
From that interview we take the following:—*. . .
And, strange to say, he added, to-day a man gave me a
~ copy of the Christmas number of a certain Dublin
weekly which has been about the bitterest opponent of
The " Playboy,” containing an article on our plays,
which says that ‘ The Building: Fund,” ‘ The Eloquent
empsey,” and ‘The Shadow of the Glen,’ should be
issed off the stage, and especially the two former, which

re both by Mr. Boyle.”” . :

Dublin weekly '’ is the LEADER. Quite
nner, if the “Freeman’’ reported him
nguidly declares that “a man gave me,
 fact is that Mr. W. B. Yeats came to
n Saturday expressly to get a copy

stage. In the course of our reference o “ The Build-

of the issue containing the article he refers to! He is
hard set to get Mr. Boyle into Mr. Synge’s boat. It is
simply quibbling, and cheap quibbling at that, on the
part of Mr. Yeats to represent that an article in the
LeApEr stated that “ The Building Fund ”’ and the
“ Eloquent Dempsey ’’ should be hissed off the stage.
The article referred to was a very clever and amusing
skit, headed “ As Others See us.”” It was supposed to
have been written by a Cockney in Dublin to a friend
in England, and covered more than one topic. ~ The
writer is represented as not having left Dublin for the
interior of Ireland. As he says—and the sentence gives
the note of the whole skit—“In these long, dark
winter nights I'm told an Englishman is a fair mark
for a bullet in the wild districts.”” This Englishman is
represented as having gone to the Abbey Theatre—“And
I may tell you, my boy, that each time I slipped a
revolver into my pocket, for there’s no harm in being
on the safe side, is there?’ The name of the theatre
puzzled him; it had a churchy sound. “I don’t know
whether the priests insisted on it (I’m sure they’re at
the back of this business), but there you are!”” Though
the skit may be in the recollection of our readers, we
will quote a few more sentences from it—*The first
piece was some legendary business about the ancient
Irish. It was by a chap called Yeats. He’s the local
Kipling, I believe, who writes poetry stuff which he
calls plays, but I'm giggered if I can understand them.”
Again :—“ During the intervals, Yeats and some other
long-haired fellows go behind the scenes. They make a
great business of going up and down a little staircase,
stopping to talk on it, when half way up, and so on,
showing off, in fact, but I suppose they think this
bucks up the bis a bit.”” The supposed Cockney in Ire-
land then refers to two of Mr. Boyle’s plays, and Mr.
Synge’s “Shadow of the Glen.”” Towards the end of
the skit he says:— It’s my belief that if the Liverpool
Trish got inside the Abbey Theatre, they’d tear the
place down, and throw the actors into the Liffey.”

Well, Mr. Yeats must be hard pressed for a point
when he tortures this skit--which he came to our office
in search of—into an article on “our plays’’ which
says that two of Mr. Boyle’s pieces “should be hissed
off the stage.”  Perhaps this is only an example
of “exaggeration apropos!”

Now, if it is the game of Master Yeats to use the
LEADER in an effort to extricate himself and his friends
from the ugly position in which the withdrawal of Mr.
Boyle has landed them, we may be excused for having
a say in the matter. In our issue of January 27th,
1906, wo wrobe an editorial notice of The Eloquent
Dempsey.”’ -Here is one sentence from that article—
«We can recommend ‘ The Eloquent Mr. Dempsey ’ to
our readers; they will enjoy it. It is a great advance
on the same author’s  Building Fund.’”’  Again—“The
play certainly is very entertaining, and is well worth
seeing.”’.

We do not make an index for the LEADER, so we spent

upwards of half an hour looking for an editorial refer-

ence to the  Building Fund.”’- Wo find it in our issue

of October 14th, 1905. We find that though our views
of thel play were not very complimentary, we did not
attack it, nor suggest that it should be hissed - he
ing Fund,” we said:—"TIt was not a comedy, nor
tragedy, nor a farce. We did not think much

THE LEADER.

3 It was sordid and morbid, without any traces of thab

> passionate sincerity that may make a study of sordid-
ness healthy and interesting. It was unnatural and
unreal.”  We do not like “The Building Fund,” but
wo did not object to its being staged, nor did we say
that it should be hissed.

2 __“Expectant ”’ writes with reference to Beamish and
Crawford’s proposed club:— Father O’Leary’s ad-
mirable letter and the article of ¢ Anti-Club,’ have by
this time focussed public attention on the merits of
Messrs. Beamish and Crawford’s application to have
2 drinking club attached to the brewery premises for
the purpose of supplying strong liquor to thirsty em-
ployees and their friends at late hours of night. A
discussion took place this week at the Cork Police Court
as to the date of re-hearing this case, and Sir Edward
Fitzgerald upset the calculations of some of its pro-
moters by having it adjourned from 2nd to 11th inst.
Next Monday (11th inst.) will, therefore, be a field
day at the Police Court, and it will be seen whether

> the cause for which Father O’Leary so justly and so

strongly pleads will prevail against the machinations of

a pompous brewer who seeks to gratify his vanity by

setting this trap for his dupes. In the latest discussion

Mr. Tilson, J.P., says he is not interested in the

liquor business. How very pretty! Everybody knows

that Mr. Tilson is the retired ex-manager of Sir John

Arnott’s brewery, and that the golden egg he now pos-

sesses had its genesis in that concern. It would be

interesting to trace what  paternal’’ solicitude M.

Robert Day and Mr. M. P. Buckley have for the appli-

| cation. Both of them are busy men, and the hum-

? drum academic proceedings of a Police Court have, on

ordinary occasions, little attraction for them. Yet the

man in the street remarks that when the toesin is

sounded such important beings as harness-makers, out-

fitters, and stockbrokers, respond readily. Why? It

is possible that your readers may be apprised of the

reason why when the case is disposed of. As for the
result, well, nons verrons.”

Some Burgesses of the Inn’s Quay Ward are not quite
satisfied with the performance of Alderman Cole, at
a recent meeting of the Dublin Corporation, when the
church crisis in France was discussed. A requisition is
being signed by Burgesses of the ward repudiating his
action and calling upon ‘him “to resign the trust he has
so abused.”’

Our reverend friend, whose £5 we have not yet spent
on the nucleus of a library, writes to us:—I trust
you will pardon me for further trespassing on your
time, but as your contributors are including books on
religion and other subjects of general interest in their
nucleus-of-a-library list, I wish to offer a word of expla-
nation. There is a school in each of three distriots in
this parish. In each district I hope to be able to raise
a sum of five pounds, so that my library, when com-
pleted, will cost £15. The first five pounds is to be
devoted to the purchase of the Irish-Ireland books; the
second to general literature, and the third to religious.
% If you care to develop this suggestion in your
4 paper, I shall be very pleased to send you another five
pounds. For the present, I will only say I am truly
grateful for your kindness, and beg to submit a plan
for “laying out’’ £5 on general literature.

FEBRUARY 9, 1907.

The principal feature of the February number of the
“Irish Rosary’’ is a trenchant and masculine article
“On the causes of the Paralysis among French Catho-
lics during the present Religious Crisis.”” The writer
of the article is a French Ecclesiastic now living in
exile in England. There is a freshness and vigour
about the article which make it very interesting read-
ing; it is a personal or individual view of the situation
put forth with almost breezy freedom. The author
says :— Once more I ask is France a Catholic country?
If T may be allowed to make a comparison, I say, is
she as Catholic as Belgium, Germany, or Ireland?
Unbhesitatingly and emphatically, T answer, no. Is she
as Catholic as Italy, Portugal, or Spain of the near
future? Perhaps she is, but in admitting this I by no %‘
means intend to extol her. Let me modify the ques- ‘
tion in this wise: Is France in anyway Christian, Re-

ligous, or even Deist? I am compelled to answer

emphatically again, that officially, formally, and even "
avowedly, my country is nothing of the kind.”” Further g
on in the article the author says:—I have yet another 1

question to ask: Is the representative authority, that
is, President Fallidres, the premier, Clemenceau, and

the other members of the Senate and house of deputies, 4,
really representative of the: vast majority of the French .
nation? T make bold to afirm that undoubtedly it is.

A contrary opinion, I know, is held by many, but to
me it appears to be well meant rather than well
founded.”” The author regrets the weakness of the Catho-
Jic press; he sighs for Louis Veuillot. “Would to God
he were still living, and able to handle his brave pen
once more—a better and more efficient weapon than
any defensive arms! Bub it is no use sighing and re-
gretting. He is no more, and has had no Catholic suc-
cessor, although valiant and disinterested men are not
wanting in the Catholic journals. But verily, both in
the Press and in Parliament, and among the higher
clergy of the Church, we are destitute of men able to
lead the multitude and put our enemies to rout.”

However, it would be scarcely fair to our contem-
porarary to quote much more from this vigorous and
frank article which is well worth reading and re-read-
ing. Indeed, a study of it may cause some wholesome
searching of hearts amongst Irish Catholics, who, on
oxamining their position, may find that they are drift-
ing with the currents rather than manfully fighting for
their cause.

There are various other interesting articles in the
February “Rosary.”” Amongst the articles on books,
is one on “ Tom O’Kelly.”” The writer of the review
of the book, commences by saying:—“This work by
Mr. Moran has not received the attention it deserves e
from the Press.”” We think we are right in saying that i
the receipt of a copy was not even acknowledged by AT
the “Simply Deplorable.”” Oh, liberty, what petty
boycotting is committed in thy name! The review of
the volume in the “ Rosary ’’ is headed : ** Tom O’Kelly
as a Temperance Book.” The writer says: “As a
_temperance book ¢ Tom' O'Kelly ’ is one of the best we
have seen for a long time. The author has uscd the
scalpel with deadly effect in dissecting the ravages that
the demon of drink is making amongst all classes of
the community.’’

of which is £170 per year. This place is in Wales The
Postma..sber-Geum‘al—or, rather, perhaps his immediato

fe;elmgs‘——a,re‘ careful to intimate that a knowledge of

M. Delany, M.P., recently questioned the P.M.G.,
M. Sydney Buxton, in reference to the appointment of
& messenger, as he has been so described, in connection
with the secretary’s office in Dublin to the Postmaster-
The salary of the post is stated to
be £124 per year, with an allowance of £13 in respect
of rent, and a further allowance as against office ex-
penses of £18 per year equivalent to £155 per year all
told. We find that the maximum wages of a messenger
attached to the secretary’s office is 38s. per week, equi-
valent to £98 16s. per year—or an increase upon the
total of some £56 4s. p:r year to the appointee in this
case. Not a bad increase by any means. How many
in the ranks of sorting clerks and telegraphists, encoun-
tering the hardships of irregular hours and divided
duties, would be glad to grasp at such preferment. Mr.
Delany, M.P., sought a return giving the particulars of
service qualifications; and records of the sorting clerks
and telegraphists who applied for the vacancy at Kil-
lucan. But the P.M.G. bluntly informed Mr. Delany
that all applications were carefully considered.  Of
course they were; perhaps most carefully. And he was
not prepared to give the return suggested; not likely,
indeed !

following:—A few months ago when going into
Limerick from the Junction, I travelled with a Cro:s-
Channel passenger who praised the liberality of the
G. S. and W. Company in a manner that surprised me,
and proved his case by producing a cheap ticket—Lion-
don to Limerick—with a memo. attached which had
‘enabled him to go round by Waterford without one
penny excess. This he told me had been done at
Kingsbridge, on presenting his ticket to be excessed,
instead of collecting the excess he had proposed to pay!
He added that his custom was limited to one annua!
trip to Limerick. I had taken a Killarney tourist
ticket at a rate something like double what che G. S.
Co. got out of the Cross-Channe] tickct. Having to
call at Limerick, I got another ticket at the Junction,
intending to join the main line at Charleville—which is
permitted on ordinary tickets. Being refused, and hav-
ing to pay again, I asked for refund on the distance
botween Limerick Junction and Charleville. The reply
I got from the Company was that, ‘No allowance
whatever could be made in respect of any deviation on
a tourist ticket, they being issued at such low rates.’

To other words, if I had gone to Waterford instead of

There is quite enough about the Abbey affair in this
issue without our using up space with remarks about
the discussion that took place on Monday night.  The
initial mistake, or outrage, was in the staging of the
play at all; though, from one point of view, it is
perhaps as well that the public at this early stage
should have had an example of what the Abbey coterie
considers “drama.” A few of our friends have won-
dered that we published the column advertisement
or rather manifesto, which appeared in our last issue,
and which was also published in various other papers.
Perhaps someone would tell us why we should not have
published it, for we could find no adequate reason for
refusing it.

We did not agree with those who absolutely refused a
hearing to the play on the occasion of its second per-
formance. We went to hear the play, but we could not
hear it, and it certainly would establish a dangerous
precedent if a few people were allowed to arrogate to
themselves the censorship of public performances. The
logical extension of such tactics would enable a band of
wreckers to go round with a few tin cans, and prevent
anyone being heard that they chose to resolve should be
suppressed. The Abbey Theatre never drew a popular
audience ; the people who frequented it might be called
students, and surely they might be allowed to judge for
themselves at the second performance of the piece in
public. They were not. On Monday, whether the oppo-
sition was organised or not, the fact remained that a
section of the audience had made up their minds that
the play was not to be heard. ~ We must tako these
things in connection with the circumstances attending
them. The play had only been heard once, and as the
management did not see its way to take it off the stage,
those who went to see it on Monday should have been
allowed to hear it subject, of course, to the boos and
hisses, and occasional and even long interruptions of
any of the audience whose indignation was aroused by
the silly travesty on Irish life.

For our own part, we felt it difficult to get hot over
this affair in view of the fact that we have grown
quite weary of showing up the immoral and degrading
imported atrocities that appear frequently at the Gaiety
and at the Royal. During the last six yeais we have
been off and on attacking dirty imported plays that
dirty Dublin likes, and the pious *“ Freeman’’ has not
done its duty in the cause of Ireland and decency in this
connection.

An atrocity like the “Playboy,’’ presentcd to the
sparse and critical audiences that frequent the Abbey,
is a different thing altogether from the popular “ Kitty
Greys’’ and “Spring Chickens”’ that are calculated
to lower and contaminate the morals of the imita-
tive, the unthinking and the Anglicised ; and yet they

are'not hooted down, and the pious “ Freeman’’ adver-
tises them, and is afraid to attack them. The “Play-
boy ”’ may now be regarded as killed. * Is it too much
to hope that one resu%; of the healthy indignation that
was' aroused over it will be that the eyes
of the people—and particularly of the pious “ Freeman’’
—will be opened to the iniquities and demoralising ten-
dency of such things as “Kitty Grey,” “The Spring
Chicken,’’ and the like, and that the next time one of
these things comes to Dublin that the “ Freeman '’ and
the “Evening Telegraph’’ will at last do their duty
towards these sort of performances.

duced by the Society are “ The Fiddler’s House,” a play

by Miss Alice Milligan.

CORRESPONDENCE:

‘Drumbaragh, Kells,
February 2nd, 1907.

Str,—I hope you will allow me to state that you

draw a wrong conclusion from my letter to the “ Free:
man’s Journal,”’ in reply to Mr.. Stephen Gwynn’s
speech, when you say: Evidently Mr. Sweetman is
getting nervous of being Vice-I’rgsulent of an organisa-
tion of which Alderman Cole is one of the loudest
official spokesmen.’’ :

T look on it as an honour to be elected Vice-President
of the National Council ; and, although I disclaim being
a leader of any party, I boast of being a strong and
enthusiastic supporter of the Sinn Fen party.

I agree with Mr. Yeats that we have now no leader
of the Irish race at home and abroad, bpt I think the
Irish Nation is getting on very well without one, as
shown by the disapproval with which Mr. Synge’s new
play was received at the Abbey 'Fhgntre, and the sup-
port the Irish Trade Mark is receiving throgghout the
whole country. In spite of cavillers, tho Irish Nation
is marching on.—Yours truly,

JOHN SWEETMAN.

THE COMEDY OFF THE STAGE.

HE developments in connection with the staging
of Mr. Synge’s alleged “ comedy ** at the Abbey
Theatre, are creating highly comic situations in all
quarters.  The all-creeds, all-classes aspiration, or
bogey, has not stood the shock. The “‘Irish°
Times,”” seeing what the ‘‘Freeman’’ was up to,
became the champion of freedom! Some Trinity
College roughs went down to the erstwhile Morgue
and, apparently, did everything but throw mud—
at which they are pastmasters—in defence of, of
all things in the world, freedom for the presentation
of ideas! In this particular conflict for freedom for
‘“art,”” England’s Faithful Garrison have tended in-
stinctively to go to one side, and the mere Irish have
tended to go to the other; and the mere Irish, as we
know, or as we are told, are nothing if not an artistic
people.  What is called “organised opposition !’ has
been glaring at a howling mob of Trinity College cads
who formed themselves into the first line of defence of
“ freedom of judgment,” and who probably were even
more at home in the art of spelling than they were in
the world of ideas! Of those of the “ organised opposi-
tion *’ who have been prosecuted and fined, one of them
is the father of Mr. Colum, the author cf “ The Land,”’
which was one of the first successful plays staged at the
Abbey Theatre, and another was Mr. Piaras Beaslaoi, a
promising TIrish author and literary prize-winner
at the Oireachtas; whiist the only champion of
“ freedom of judgment,’”’ who, up to the time of writ-
ing, has been fined for his efforts in the cause of art,
is a man, or boy, by the name of Persse, a “young
blackguard,’’ the magistrate called him. He is said to
be a Trinity College student, and there is no circum-
stantial reason to doubt the allegation: “he struck a
policeman in the interests, no doubt, of freedom of
judgment,”” and for the welfare of “the soul of the
nation.”” He was striking the policeman almost at the |
very time that the patriotic author of “Cathleen Ni
Houlihan *’ was pointing out Mr. Beaslaoi to another
limb of the law as a unit of the ‘“ organised opposition **
who liad earned prosecution in the interests of the
“soul of the nation.”
Tt is high comedy all the time, and there is no trace
of extravaganza or farce about it, for all the characters
in the comedy off the stage are in deadly earnesi. M. %
Persse, the man who assaulted the police, was, as a
result of the forces working in this exztraordinary
comedy off the stage, pushed up on a pedestal upon
which is inseribed “ Freedom of Judgment,”” and Mr
Colum’s father, and Mr. Piaras Beaslaoi, were dnyeu
by the same forces into the portion of the field railed
off for the “ organised opposition.”’ i
And notwithstanding all the row and the police, Mr.
Synge told a representative of ‘Buf that the play
means nothing in particular, that it is not intended to
ropresent Irish life as it is lived, that he had no object | -
in’ writing it beyond pleasing himself, that the girls
making love to the parricide were brought in to throw
out the humour of the situation; and that the play was.
made to amuse! One very humorous thing about Mr,
Synge is that he carries a Morgue, rather than a brain,
on his shoulders, and when the grey matter of the
Murgué moves, it throws off “ comedies.” J Sl
But the anti-Irish-Irishmen, the Planters, an
importations, assumed that Mr. Synge had .a
rather than a comic Morgue: on his shoulders, -
accepted the “comedy ’” which they had not h

-Playboy.”’

seen ‘as ‘a portrayal of objective truth. They grabbed
ab the “comedy ’’ of the Parricide under the auspices
of the Irish National Theatre, and as ia representative
of life in the West of Treland, and, accordingly, they
stood by  freedom of judgment,”’ obeying the same in-
stinet in doing so as they obey ‘when they cheer any-
thing the highly intellectual Mr. Walter Long says.
Mr. Synge’s ‘‘ comedy,” if played in the delectable
Potteries in England by Mr. Walter Long, supported
by Mike McCarthy, Mr. Carson, Captain Wade-
Thompson, and Mr. Campbell, K.C., would convince
the virtuous minds of the Tories of the Potteries that
they would be grievously neglecting their moral duty
if they did not oppose any further concession of legisla-
‘ive or administrative power to the Christy Mahons and
their female admirers of the Western Isle. And yet
Mr. Synge was all the while writing without any parti-
cular motive; he was merely writing to please himself
and to make the people laugh. Under the circum-
stances Mr. Synge’s laugh suggests O’Connell’s remark
about some person’s smile reminding him of the sun-
light dancing on the brass plate of a coffin.

A county or a country cannot, unfortunately, take
a criminal libel action against a “comedian’’ who in-
tended to make its people laugh! If we worked up a
sketch composed of such ingredients as a murderer, a
fool, a forger, a harlot, a drunkard, an adulterer, a
Freemason, an Orangemen, and such like choice speci-
mens of West Britons, and if, to heighten the humour
of the sketch, to make the matter an extravaganza, we
called all the male blackgnards after Mr. Yeats, Mr.
Synge, and their friends, and all the female bad charac-
ters after the female relatives and friends of these
gentlemen, would they object? What would they say
if we told them that in objecting to the names we gave
our extravaganza that was meant to make them laugh,
that they were outraging literary freedom and the
“ freedom of judgment,’”” that for our part we might
have called the low women any other names, and that
they were not really intended to represent Mr. Yeats’
women relatives and friends, and that for the rest we
did not “care a rap ’’ whether they felt pained or not?
We suspect that under the circumstances we would hear
very little from Mr. Yeats about his and his relatives’
““ mental servitude,”” but would rather be the recipients
of a writ for criminal libel. But a country cannot take
a criminal libel against Messrs. Synge and Yeats; and
when, feeling its impotence before what it considers a
painful slander, the country, or some of it, howls, the
author of Cathleen Ni Houlihan, with a yell of ‘ free-
dom of judgment,” calls in the police; the author
of Cathleen Ni Houlihan does more: he points out
Mr. Beaslaoi to one of the policemen and causes him to
be arrested, and goes and gives evidence in the Court
the following morning against the father of the author
of ‘“ The Land.”’

It is comic to strain at a gnat and to swallow a camel.
Here we are during the last six years, almost a voice in
the wilderness, crying out against such dirty things as
‘“ Zaza,”’ ‘‘ Sapho,” ‘‘Kitty Grey,”” the ‘‘ Spring
Chicken’” and Co., and now we find “Pink,”” all of a
sudden, deepening in colour at the mention of “The
‘When we first came on the scene we found
““ Pink’s >’ big brother, the ‘‘ Freeman,’’ puffing an in-
decent music hall performance. Iowever, we now hope
to see “Pink ’’ turning purple at the next visit from
‘“Kitty Grey,”” or ‘‘ The Spring Chicken!’’ What,
after all, is a silly and palpable slander on the Irish
people, or a play with coarse unabashed dialogue, and
maybe an odd dab of brutal filth presented to a small
and critical section of the public, compared in iniquity
with the 3nora1 evil, the moral pestilence, represented
by the gaily dressed and'less than half-dressed vice and
suggestiveness that does duty for drama so often at the
Gaitety and the Royal before uncritical and imitative
well-dressed rabbles of both sexes from Pembroke, Rath-
mines, and Donnybrook, and which the virtuous * Free-
man’’ refrains from denouncing as it should denounce
them. “Stone the ‘Playboy’ and let ‘Kitty Grey’
o free,”’ is evidently the motto of the ‘‘ Freeman ’’!

“French Frenchman ’’ in France, a “ German Swiss’’
in Switzerland, a “Jap Japanese’’ in Japan? There is a
narrow and a broad view of an “Irish Irelander.” I am of
the opinion that an “Irish Irelander’’ ought to be the
equivalent of their coiresponding class in the above
States and America. What has made these nations
so powerful and rich, so intellectual and self-reliant, so
practical as well as ideal? It is a combination of many
things rather than any one thing in particular. Their
political freedom has helped them to success, but it has
not caused them to succeed. Other nations with equal
freedom are growing smaller and poorer! It cannot
be denied that the broad and general explanation of
their greatness and success is the expansiveness of their
national vision, their recognition and knowledge of the
world outside their own frontiers. They give their
share and surplus to the outer world, and they take
back to themselves all that they need or admire from
the outer world. Their policy is to give and take, to
export; and import material, intellectual, spiritual and
pleasurable things.  They think and work for them-
selves first, and for humanity afterwards. “Sinn
Fein ”’ is their policy, but not the “Sinn Fein ’’ policy
of the ostrich in a sand storm, or of the historical imbe-
cile prisoner of the Bastille.

- Unless we are infinitely inventive, we must be con-
tent to imitate. We must judge by comparison. A
successful athlete is considered very famous until it is
found that others can beat him with ease. We are apt
to congratulate ourselves on our superior knowledge
until we learn our own ignorance compared to others.
‘We must be prepared to admit the good in others and
the evil in ourselves as readily as we oxpect others to
admit the evil in themselves and the good in us.

spreading mental disease and material failure wherever

In ono matber we must face an ugly fact and act
accordingly ; it is in the matter of history—the greatest
educator of character that we possess. We must
take it for granted that an earnest, sincere partisan, or
sectarian, will not write full and complete history; he
may not tell lies, but he will suppress facts, causes,
explanations, and the like. It is better not to read his-
tory at all than to live in a ““fool’s paradise ”’ created
by one-sided history. For instance, 'take the Reforma-
tion period, read the account of it written by the truth-
ful, but partisan Cobbet; next read the account of it
by the truthful, but partisan D’Aubigné! What a
difference it makes in our conception of the period, in
our judgment of its principal actors, in our sym-
pathies! Historical truth is not enough, the full his-
torical truth is necessary, hence a wise man, a fair man,
above all, a public man should read both sides, all
things to the contrary notwithstanding.

COMPETITION FOR DISTINCTIONS.

HE most important changes in the conditions of

the competition for distinctions in 1908, are I.,

the admission of all students to the special papers; II.,
the greater freedom allowed to candidates for Exhibi-
bitions and prizes; III., the abolition of third-class
Exhibitions; and IV., the method of determining the

number of first-class and of second-class Exhibitions.
I.—In addition to the Pass and Honours papers, there
will be, as last year, special papers of a more searching
character in the main subjects. To be qualified for an
Exhibition or Prize in any course, a candidate must
pass with Honours in three subjects, two of which must
be the main subjects of that course. The net Honours
and special marks of the main subjects, together with
the net IHonour marks in two other subjects, which
may be called secondary, and the excess over 50 per
cent. in English, will count towards an Exhibition or
prize. So far there has been no change as compared
with last year. But rule 45, which enumerates the
main subjects of the several courses, contains this im-
portant sentence:—* Students may take any of the
special papers, but only the marks (as determined by
rule 44) obtained on the special papers of two main
subjects in one of the above courses, will be added to
the honour marks counting towards Exhibitions or
prizes.”’ These words effect some very important
changes. Last year none but those who had entered
for a certain course were allowed to take the special
papers. Hence, no student could aspire to a medal or
prize in Latin, for instance, unless he also took Greek
and entered for the Classical course; nobody could hope
for a medal in Irish unless he also took French or Ger-
man, and entered for Modern Literary course (Divi-
sion I); nobody could have a shot for a medal in Alge-
bra unless he also took Geometry or Science; and,
queerest of all, a senior grade mathematician may feel
equally strong in Algebra, Geometry and Trigonometry,
yet he should, for the sacred cause of specialisation,
select two of them in advance, and he would not be
allowed to compete for the medal in the third subject.
The result was that there was no guarantee that.medals
and composition prizes were given to the best students.
“Medals for mediocrity,’”’ was the term applied to them
by teachers generally. Though the several suggestions
offered by the headmasters with a view to reduce the
iniquity of the courses, were refused by the Board last
jyear, it is some comfort to find some of them adopted
for 1908. According to my reading of the rules, all
students may take the special papers; hence, no student:
within the prescribed limits of age, is debarred from:
competing for a medal or composition prize in an;
subject. iy s
II.—No notice is required of a student’s intention
to take a special paper or to seek an Exhibition

prize in a specified course. The vagueness of
of the rule, together with the deliberate omissio:
rule 23 (May declaration) of the words req

ment ; but one cannot be sure without an authoritative
decision of the Board. Supposing my view to be cor-
rect, then every sfudent could make the most of his
knowledge in all the subjects he was studying during
the year, and, apparently, he would be placed after
~ the examination in the course for which, according
to his answering, be was best suited. This would
minimise the glorious uncertainty of the turf that was
‘brought into the Intermediate system quite recently.
III.—There will be only two classes of Exhibifions
having the following valucs:—Senior Grade £40 and
£20, Middle Grade £25 and £15, Junior Grade £15
and £10. The total number of Exhibitions is to be
ot more than one-third nor less than one-fourth of

removal of third-class Exhibitions, nor should I object
to the diminution of the total number of Exhibitions.
Latterly, too many Exhibitions were given, especially
in Junior Grade. Boys were branded by the Interme-

were, in many cases, of rather mediocre ability and
attainments. £5 Exhibitions are not more deserving

decent reason in favour of its abolition. Then the rule
that regulates the number of Exhibitions, i.e., not
more than one-third nor less than one-fourth of those
that qualify in a given course is not fair. The same
proportion is given in all courses and in all grades;
whereas every headmaster knows that there are several

details.

Prosecutions for trivial acts. In other words, to reduce
the figure recorded by the barometer of disturbance.
Among these vexatious prosecutions there can be no
doubt he understood prosecutions for having Irish names
on carts, or on applications for dog-licences.  If I re-
member aright, these vexatious prosecutions were in
full swing far into the reign of King Bryce the first,
and probably would sfill be continued if cases of the
proper character presented themselves. It is to be
hoped, however, now that the Irish public have got a
broad hint from Mr. Bryce’s speech that it is owing to
the benignity of the peace-loving, disturbance-hating,
heaven-sent Liberals that they need not fear vexatious
prosecutions for having their proper names in their
own language on their own carts; it is to be hoped, I
say, that they will act up to the spirit of this new depat-
ture. The Dublin Corporation, to give them their due,
have given a useful lead in this direction. A few
private individuals have done something to test the sin-
cerity of the professions of peace-loving Liberal politi-
cians. But it remajs still sternly true that the over-
whelming majority of personal and place names in Ire-
land continue to be written and printed in an Anglicised
form, often barbarous, generally corrupt, and, for the
most part, hideous to the eye and discordant to the ear,

There is a great deal to be done in the direction of
getting the vast body of the Irish public to use the cor-
rect Irish form of their Christian names and surnames,
and of the places in which they live, or by which they
are immediately surrounded. A revolution of this
nature would advance the cause of Irish Ireland in
general, and of the language movement in particular,
to an enormous extent. Au Irishman who uses only
the corrupt Anglicised form of his name cannot feel the
full force of legitimate pride of race. ~When we use
our own proper namts undoctored by Anglican physic,
we feel that the confiscation of our ancestral patrimony
has not robbed us of everything. ~Why should any
Irishman be content to use only an Anglicised and bar-
barous form of his name? Very often the answer has
been because the majority of Irishmen do not know the
correct form of their true native names. This answer,

" in the past often reasonable, is fortunately daily losing
its force; and the publication of Father Wolfe’s
“Stomnce Gaeveal 1p Sall,” which is called in Eng-
lish “Irish Names and Surnames,”” will render this
answer still more ineffective.  This book is a handy
cheap list of the Irish names and surnames which are
most commonly used at the present time in Ireland. It
does not profess to be complete, but it will serve a use-
ful purpose, and the desired completeness may be ex-
pected in dus time. As far as I can see, it is sufficiently
accurate to be used with safety by the general public. It
is not, however, my aim in this article to enter into a
critical discussion of its merits, but rather to exhort the
Irish public to make use of it as a leverage for restoring
to our people their own proper names. There are, no
doubt, some Irish names and surnames that have
nothing to spare from the point of view of eup!mny, but,
in general the genuine Trish forms of our Trish names
are infinitely more euphonious than their Anglmsed
forms, I have always considered the Teutonic sur-

AN IRISH: SCHOOL FOR WEST KERRY.

IT is now pretty generally admitted that attendance
for a few evenings a week at. a Gaelic League class,
even though continued for several years, rarely succeeds
in making a fluent Irish speaker of one born with an
English tongue in his mouth, unless he has frequent
opportunity of meeting and conversing in Irish with
native speakers of that language. That so many of
our students never get out of the “ca pe apsi’ style
of conversation is not very much to be wondered at.
Irish is a language abounding in idiom and presenting
many difficulties to the learner. A thorough know-
iedge of it can be acquired only from listening to
correct speakers and afterwards imitating them. How
can the student of Irish, living (say) in an Anglicised
town, and hearing correct Irish spoken only while he is
listening to the teacher of the local Craobh;how can
he succeed in becoming an Irish speaker? Assuredly
the task he has set himself is no ordinary one, and ‘he
must be possessed of much grit and an uncommon
amount of perseverence if he does succeed.

My own contention has always been that a three-
weeks’ residence in an Irish-speaking district would be
more beneficial to a student of Irish than would three
attendances weekly and continued for a whole year at
the classes of a Craobh whose surroundings are English.
For this reason I think it is a pity that Summer
Schoo's for the teaching of Irish have been abandoned.
They brought many holiday-seekers into the Irish-
speaking districts, and were doubly a gain to the lan-
guage: Irish gained in respect among the inhabitants
of these districts, and visitors ‘had an excellent means
cf improving their conversational knowledge of Irish.

True, we have now an Irish Training College in each
of the four provinces. Unfortunately, for one reason
or another, students are not availing themselves in any
considerable numbers of the facilities afforded them by
these colleges.  The Irish School at Ring ought to
become very popu'ar with students of the language,
provided the authorities of the school have Irish, and
that only, taught.

A good deal has been lately said in the ¢ Kerryman,”
a paper published in Tralee, on the subject of an Irish

School for West Kerry. There was much discussion;

the main point on which the writers disagreed being

the necessity for, and t_he most suitable spot, to choose.

THE RUINED “ABBEY.”

HE last rays of the setting sun lay tenderly upon
the shattered walls. Sadly yet caressingly they
flitted among the ruined pillars. The green moss and
clinging ivy were all around; grass grew among the
long untrodden pavements. Not far away the limpid
Liffey reflected the dying rays. There was a stillness
in the air. A mute spectator gazed upon the scene.
Many emotions struggled in his breast for mastery. It
was, indeed, one of those moments that come now and
again into the lives of all of us; one of those precious
moments when even the least susceptible must yield
himself a-victim to the mystic influences roused by the
memory of the faded past. The twilight hour is po-
tent in itself, but when one stands in contemplation of
an ancient shrine where once the sacred lamp has
flamed, where voices now long hushed have chanted
poems, where gorgeous spectacles or simpler scenes have
filled the vision of a bye-gone age, feelings that even
a poet may mnot gauge throb in the swelling heart.
We are standing in the graveyard of the undying dead.
So felt the stranger on this summer eve. Having
paused awhile in involuntary homage to the unseen
spirit all-pervading, he advanced slowly and reveren-
tially through the ruined fane. No chattering guide,
no desecrating guide-book intruded harshly on his
thoughts. He needed none.  Voices from the outer
world must surely grate discordantly on ears awake to
catch the whispers of the past. What need to tell that
this was once the stalls. Here sat the worshippers
olad in sad habiliments of white and black; here, too,
the devotees in the much-varied fabrics of their cult.
From this hallowed precinct rose the adulation offered
with unceasing adoration by the chosen few. For not in
these stalls did the rabble dare to mix with those of
high degree. Those alone whose faith was strong and
pure, who saw beneath the symbols to the undying
truth, might here assist at solemn service. Alas, time
hath dealt none too gently with these once-splendid
seats. Decay is over all.  The extravagance of arb
whereat once a people marvelled, has, in the lapse of
ears, faded into mere oblivion.

And look! here are the stalls where sat the chief man
and his choir. What memories crowd in upon the
mind.  Hero sat the poet in his robe of black, his
intense soul gazing through its mirrors into the illimit-
able beyond. = Ever was he ready to go behind the
scenes of outward show into the mystic regions bounded
by the wings of mere materialism. A man skilled in

the law withal, and a rigid enforcer of the discipline.

The Brethren sat here, too, obedient to the rule. They
tell that now and then some restless spirit, chaffing
for a less confined life, broke from the cloistered walls

THE LEADER.

FeBRUARY 9. 1907.

and sought the world without. Restless, indeed, and
ill-attuned to a life of solitude and self-contemplation,
must have been such a one to hasten to the land over
the hills and far away.

Yonder broken arch, and steps all worn away with
constant passing of eager feet, are all that stay to mind
us of the path by which these men of olden time entered
and left the choir. Naught remains to show where
sab the music-makers, but we know that from a place
apart sweet strains arose mingling with the half-tones
of the worshippers. Fancy still may trace where sat
the chief woman in her solemn robes. A great
foundaton was this ancient pile, and a Sisterhood was
banded here, joined in the vows of mutual admira-
tion. Where * be ye all now, victims of the
great comedy? Hath oblivion been your fate, or do
yo perchance continue your mystic rites in some far
distant world ?

The wayfarer turns, and seeks the place beyond,
set apart in ancient times for the poor and
lowly. Overhead, supported by the pillars which still
stand, one may yet discern the outlines of the gallery.
No records live to tell us who came there. We find it
hard to people once again those lofty seats. Perchance
at no time were they thronged. From such a dizzy
height none could look down with aught but trembling
into the space below. Doubless a few might not enter
below, yet would not mingle with the herd,
took up their places here. A studious contemplation of
the edifice shows that the audience was in chief
composed of the elect. Time has not so far destroyed
with his remorseless hand the handiwork of those who
built the temple that we may not see the outlines of the
rails which kept the rabble from the chosen. Beyond
these rails stretching to the ruined walls there lies a
little space. Hither flocked the curious, the poor—yea,
the unbeliever. For doubt it not, unbelievers thronged
to this fane even as they have done to others, and it is
recorded in musty tomes how, at one time, at least,
those who came to scoff remained to pay.

The twilight deepens. Through the shattered win-
dow the moon may be discerned, flecked ever and anon
with fleecy cloud. Before us lies the platform whereon
princely pageants have been displayed—aye, the sordid
scenes. Princes and beggarmen, heroes and criminals,
have passed to and fro upon it. Tigures of substance
and of shady, of good repute and ill have come and gone.
Strange scenes have here been witnessed ;light and dark-
ness have held sway. The scene-shifter has, indeed, been
busy in this ancient pile. ~Now grass-grown are the
mosaic paths, broken the stately shafts, crumbled the
ancient walls. The Abbey is a ruin!

How came it thus, my masters? Who can tell? Lost
in the twilight of the forgotten past is the sad history
of the Abbey. We hear if we listen to the whispering
walls murmurs of a great tumult.  Tales of how the
unbelievers rose against'a creed they could not under-
stand, hints of some dark crime done in the Abbey
walls. Ah! if those walls would but speak out. If
they would but proclaim the secret locked in their
stoney breasts, what might we not learn? As it is, we -
must but guess a truth. Some say that there was
trouble through a building fund, others have heard of
a strange, unnatural deed done within the cloistered
precincts, a deed of blood.  Nothing certain do we
know. We do put sail through shadowy waters in our
pursuit, and ’tis an idle task.

Night has fallen. The stranger weary at heart with
long communing in the solitudes turns him homewarn.
He springs into a passing tram, and swiftly passes from
the region of by-gone things. The moon casts weird
shadows in the ruined temple he has left. Ghosts of
vanished men stray amid the crumbling walls. - An
uncouth shadow strikes at another with a clumsy
weapon ; shrieks and cheers and sibilant sounds sweep
along the blast. Then silence, and the passing police-
man walks unheeding by. Avis.
